sources.title: Maj. James Dearing's report (Dearing's Artillery Battalion, Pickett's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 387-391
sources.author: James Dearing
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route, id = 189683 + page; pp. 387-390 read). Dated Aug. 16, 1863. A REPORT PASS 1 DID NOT KNOW EXISTED (the battalion had been assumed Pendleton/tablet-only). Keys: arrived near the field July 2 (the Pendleton 'arrived since dusk' march chain with Eshleman); early July 3 'marched to the field of battle' into position 'on the crest of the hill immediately in front of the enemy's position'; THE ARC'S BEST UNIT-TO-UNIT SPACING STATEMENT: 'On my left and rear was Colonel Cabell's Artillery Battalion, and on my right and rear was the Washington Artillery Battalion'; daybreak sharpshooter fight, Stribling 'firing about a dozen rounds'; Maj. J. P. W. Read 'wounded in the head by a fragment of shell'; at the signal guns 'commencing firing slowly and deliberately' by battery 'at the batteries immediately in my front'; ammunition 'became completely exhausted, excepting a few round in my rifled guns'; batteries under 'a very heavy fire for upward of an hour without being able to fire a single shot'; 'about 6 p. m.' drove back an advancing infantry column (late-clock conflict, see profile); July 4 with McLaws, withdrew near sundown to Black Horse Tavern. Per-battery casualties: Stribling 3 w + 10 horses; Macon 3 k, 3 w + 8 horses; Blount 5 k&w + 12 horses; Caskie's figure NOT located in the fetched extraction (flagged, not attested-absent). Tablet conflict: the War Dept tablet has the battalion 'withdrawn from the field about 4 P. M.' vs his own 18:00 infantry-repulse statement - carried.

clockProfile.assessment: His signal-gun sequence is stated relative to the signal itself (tier A structurally, no clock given). The one stated clock, 'about 6 p. m.' for repulsing advancing infantry, sits ~2.5 h after the canonical repulse; either a genuinely later Union probe/skirmish advance or a very late clock - unresolved, envelope records it. Rule 4: display-only. (Envelope floor clipped to the schema's -120 min bound; the raw conflict is larger and lives in the editionNote.)

sources.title: Col. Henry C. Cabell's report (Cabell's Artillery Battalion, McLaws's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 374-378
sources.author: Henry C. Cabell
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 375-377 read). Keys: July 2 position 'on the edge of the wood, the right resting near the road leading from Gettysburg to Emmitsburg', dueling across ~600-700 yards at the Peach Orchard; Capt. Fraser 'dangerously wounded by the bursting of a shell' which killed two sergeants and one man; July 3 morning: McCarthy advanced 300-400 yards ahead of the skirmishers, fired twenty rounds; consolidation (Fraser's depleted command), six guns near a brick house on the Gettysburg-Emmitsburg road, Carlton + a McCarthy section 'slightly en echelon' before Pickett's division; cannonade 'about 1 p. m.' - 'For over two hours the cannonading on both sides was almost continuous and incessant, far, very far, exceeding any cannonading I have ever before witnessed'; Lt. Jennings w, Capt. Carlton w; post-assault forward move 'immediately on the road', one of Furlong's guns 'ordered to the rear' (ammunition exhausted); ~16:00 held ground 'about 700 yards from the Cemetery Hill' with orders 'to hold it till night'; withdrawn after dark. Casualty totals not in the fetched pages (tablet carries 12 k / 30 w / 4 m + 80 horses; the SS compilation page's 15 k / 57 w is the carried variant). Tablet ordnance figure: 'expended about 3300 rounds' July 2-3. PASS-3 UPGRADE: the unfetched casualty-table item is CLOSED via the compiled ANV return (or-27-2-anv-return p. 338), which prints the battalion PER-BATTERY: Carlton (Troup) 1/6=7, Fraser (Pulaski) 4/14=18, McCarthy (1st Richmond Howitzers) 2/3=5, Manly (Bty A 1st NC) 1/6=7; battalion total 8/29/-=37. Also fetched p. 385: Lt. C. W. Motes's company report (Troup Artillery) closing the battalion's July 4 posture ('the guns were not placed in position on the 4th' after Parrott ammunition exhaustion) with its own casualty line (1 k, 2 officers + 4 men w, 13 horses k).

clockProfile.assessment: Cannonade 'about 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07) with 'over two hours' duration reaching ~15:10, bracketing CA-J3A-5 cleanly. His '4 p. m.' forward-hold statement is post-assault, consistent at +7. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Lt. Col. John J. Garnett's report (Garnett's Artillery Battalion, Heth's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 652-654
sources.author: John J. Garnett
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 652-653 read). Keys: July 1 'About 11 a. m. ... orders to bring up my command to within supporting distance on the Gettysburg pike'; one battery's ammunition exhausted July 1, relieved by his rifles; July 2 'dispatched Major Richardson with the nine rifled pieces of the battalion to the hill ... opposite Cemetery Hill'; 15:00 July 2 'these pieces opened fire upon the enemy's batteries opposite, which they kept up without cessation until about thirty minutes before sunset'; JULY 3, THE ATTESTED SILENCE VERBATIM: Richardson's rifles ordered to Anderson's front 'to the right of Major Pegram's battalion' and 'placed his guns in position under fire at this point, but did not fire a single shot, having received orders to that effect'; the other six guns (four Napoleons + two howitzers) 'bore no part in these actions, although they were upon the field in readiness whenever they should be called upon'; July 4 morning reoccupied the July 2 rifle positions until night; Richardson + five rifled pieces detached to Imboden (the train guard). Tablet concurs: 'The Napoleons and Howitzers were in reserve and not actively engaged at any time.' PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 652-654 re-fetched in full 2026-07-11): THE CASUALTY GAP IS CLOSED - p. 654: 'Severely wounded, 2 enlisted men; slightly wounded, 3 enlisted men; missing (supposed to be in the hands of the enemy), 14 enlisted men', with the OR editors' footnote '*But see p. 344' pointing at the compiled return (-/5/17=22) - a report-vs-return delta of 3, both carried. The July 3 silence now has its order chain: Richardson's nine rifles were placed 'in obedience to orders from General Longstreet ... under fire at this point, but did not fire a single shot, having received orders to that effect' at 'the position held by Major-General Anderson's division, and to the right of Major Pegram's battalion'; Alexander (Military Memoirs p. 419) identifies these as the nine short-range howitzers Pendleton offered him, which he 'placed under cover close in rear of the forming column with orders to remain until sent for, intending to take them with the column when it advanced' - the silent battalion was the assault column's intended accompanying artillery. Also: July 1 Maurin detachment relieving one of Pegram's exhausted batteries; July 2 Richardson's nine rifles engaged 3 p.m. to ~30 min before sunset opposite Cemetery Hill.

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon clock statements (the day's record is a sequence of orders and an attested silence). Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Maj. D. G. McIntosh's report (McIntosh's Artillery Battalion, Third Corps reserve), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 674-677
sources.author: David Gregg McIntosh
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 675-676 read). Keys: July 1 flank fire near the Cashtown pike, guns 'enfiladed a heavy column of the enemy's infantry, formed in the railroad cut'; July 2-3 'behind a stone wall, on the range of hills to the left of the town of Gettysburg' (Seminary Ridge); July 3 sustained firing, 'not a single case' of flinching observed; July 4 'the same position was maintained with but little firing', withdrew to the stone bridge under Hill's orders that afternoon. ROUNDS EXPENDED, ITEMIZED VERBATIM: Napoleon 213 + 3-inch rifle 1,049 + Whitworth 133 = 1,395 total; casualties 24 killed and wounded, 16 captured, 38 horses disabled/killed (tablet variant: 7 k, 25 w 'of whom 16 were captured' - two accountings carried); horses 'received no corn, subsisting entirely upon grass' on the retreat.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks in the fetched pages. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Capt. Ervin B. Brunson's report (Pegram's Artillery Battalion, Third Corps reserve), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 677-679
sources.author: Ervin B. Brunson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 677-679 read). Keys: July 1 approach - 'When within 2 miles of Gettysburg, Captain Marye's section of rifled guns was run forward' and 'opened on a piece of woods to the left of the pike', 'After firing some eight or ten rounds, the pieces were limbered up' (the arc's opening-shots record); July 1 line 'ten Napoleons and seven rifled guns' forcing Union artillery 'to limber up and retire their pieces three distinct times'; July 2 repositioned 'approximately one mile right of the pike, roughly 1,400 yards from' the Union artillery, 'enfilading their batteries whenever they opened upon the batteries on our right'; July 3 'never did men behave more heroically', 'one of the most terrific' artillery fights; 'The infantry failed to dislodge the enemy from his position.' ROUNDS EXPENDED VERBATIM: 'The battalion expended in the three days' fight 3,800 rounds of ammunition' (tablet concurs). PER-DAY CASUALTIES (EC6 episode split, primary): July 1: 2 k, 8 w, 6 horses; July 2: 2 k, 7 w, 25 horses; July 3: 10 k, 37 w, 38 horses; ordnance officer mw July 1 by solid shot; retreat: ~6-8 men taken prisoner, one howitzer caisson abandoned, ~20 horses abandoned for want of shoes. Tablet casualty line (10 k / 37 w = 47) matches only the July 3 row - the tablet-vs-report accounting conflict is carried.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks in the fetched pages. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Col. R. Lindsay Walker's report (Chief of Artillery, Third Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 609-612
sources.author: R. Lindsay Walker
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 610-611 read). Keys: assumed command of the corps artillery morning of July 1; July 1: Pegram and McIntosh forward with Heth and Pender, 'in position for action on an eminence about 1 mile west of Gettysburg'; July 2: Pegram, McIntosh, Lane and part of Garnett's 'put in position, on the right of the Fairfield turnpike, about 1 mile in advance of the position of the previous day', Poague 'still farther to the right'; July 3: the batteries 'kept up an incessant fire from about 1 p. m. to the time of the advance of the infantry', nearly exhausting ammunition; Lt. Chamberlayne + four Crenshaw men captured foraging.

clockProfile.assessment: 'From about 1 p. m. to the time of the advance of the infantry' brackets the cannonade between +7-corrected 13:07 and the step-off - structurally consistent with CA-J3A-1..6. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Col. J. Thompson Brown's report (Chief of Artillery, Second Corps ANV), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 455-457
sources.author: J. Thompson Brown
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; p. 456 read). THE EWELL-WING JULY 3 PARTICIPATION RECORD: 'The First Virginia Artillery [Dance's battalion] and a portion of Carter's and Nelson's battalions engaged the enemy's batteries, in order to divert their fire from our infantry' - fire 'was well directed, and its fine effect was very noticeable', temporarily silencing Cemetery Hill artillery. July 2: Latimer's (Andrews's) battalion fire from the extreme left (Benner's Hill) 'aided by Graham's battery ... and from Dance's Watson's and Smith's jr., batteries ... on the right of our line'; Latimer withdrew three batteries against superior positioning and was mortally wounded - 'no heavier loss could have befallen the artillery of this corps.' Jones's battalion operated with the cavalry; Tanner's battery sent with the wagon train (Williamsport). This report converts the pass-1 'Ewell-wing near-silence' framing into a documented PARTIAL, diversionary participation: Dance engaged, parts of Carter/Nelson engaged, Jones detached, Latimer's battalion wrecked the evening before.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks in the fetched page. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Lt. Col. Thomas H. Carter's report (Carter's Artillery Battalion, Rodes's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 602-604
sources.author: Thomas H. Carter
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; p. 603 read). Keys: July 1 with Rodes - Page's battery fired on the infantry advancing on O'Neal from 'the foot of the high ridge'; Page's counter-battery punishment: '2 men killed, 2 mortally wounded, 26 more or less badly wounded, and 17 horses killed and disabled'; July 2: 'held in readiness to move into position, but was not engaged' (attested idle day); July 3: 'Ten rifled guns were posted on the high ridge on the right and left of the railroad cut' to 'divert the fire of the enemy's guns from Hill's and Pickett's troops' and cover Dance's three batteries; his observation of the Union guns: 'Their fire slackened, and finally ceased' - a Confederate-side sequence witness to CA-J3A-4/5 from the Oak Hill axis.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks; his slacken-then-cease observation is sequence-only (tier D) corroboration of the CA-J3A-4 -> 5 ordering from the CSA side. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Return of Casualties in the Army of Northern Virginia at the battle of Gettysburg, July 1-3 (compiled from Surg. L. Guild's detailed statement), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 338-346
sources.author: U.S. War Department compilation (Guild medical-director statement + commanders' figures)
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route, pp. 338-346 read in full). THE regiment-grain casualty primary for the assault column. Editorial headnote (p. 338, verbatim): where regimental sums disagree with brigade totals 'the discrepancy is due to disagreements between the detailed statement furnished by Surg. L. Guild ... and the numbers reported by brigade and other superior commanders'; 'the compilation can only be regarded as approximative. Several of the reports indicate that many of the "missing" were killed or wounded. Especially is this the case with Pickett's division.' Scope caution: per-regiment aggregates are k+w only for most infantry brigades (captured/missing pooled at brigade grain or absent). Key rows: Garnett 78/324/539=941 (regt k+w: 8Va 54, 18Va 87, 19Va 44, 28Va 77, 56Va 62; staff 1k); Armistead 88/460/643=1,191 (9Va 71, 14Va 108, 38Va 170, 53Va 104, 57Va 121; staff 1k); Kemper 58/356/317=731 (1Va 64, 3Va 67, 7Va 94, 11Va 109, 24Va 128; staff 1k/3w); Dearing bn 8/17/-=25 '*Not reported in detail'; TOTAL Pickett's division 232/1,157/1,490=2,888. Pettigrew's/Marshall's 190/915/-=1,105 (11NC 209, 26NC 588 [86k/502w], 47NC 161, 52NC 147; NO missing reported); Brockenbrough 25/123/-=148 (40Va 42, 47Va 48, 55Va 34, 22Va Bn 24); Archer's/Fry's 16/144/517=677 (13Ala 42, 5Ala Bn 26, 1Tenn 42, 7Tenn 23, 14Tenn 27); Davis 180/717/-=897 (2Miss 232, 11Miss 202, 42Miss 265, 55NC 198); Heth's division 411/1,905/534=2,850; Garnett's (arty) bn -/5/17=22 '*Not reported in detail' (cross-noted by the OR editors against the report's p. 654 figures); Lane 41/348/-=389 with footnote '+General Lane reports his entire loss at 660'; Scales 102/323/110=535 (13NC 126, 16NC 66, 22NC 89, 34NC 64, 38NC 79); Pender's division 262/1,312/116=1,690; McIntosh bn 7/25/-=32; Pegram bn 10/37/1=48; Wilcox 51/469/257=777 (8Ala 161*, 9Ala 58, 10Ala 104, 11Ala 75, 14Ala 48; *8Ala row rides the corps header line in the reproduction, read with care); Perry's/Lang's 33/217/205=455 (2Fla 81, 5Fla 75, 8Fla 94); Cabell bn PER-BATTERY (p. 338): Carlton 1/6=7, Fraser 4/14=18, McCarthy 2/3=5, Manly 1/6=7, total 8/29/-=37. Grand total 2,592/12,709/5,150=20,451 with the U.S. prisoner-record counter-note (12,227 names).

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Cadmus M. Wilcox's report (Wilcox's brigade, Anderson's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 616-621 (No. 537)
sources.author: Cadmus M. Wilcox
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 616-621 read in full). Dated July 17, 1863, Bunker Hill. July 3 keys: 'early in the morning, before sunrise, the brigade was ordered out to support artillery under the command of Colonel Alexander ... formed in line parallel to the Emmitsburg turnpike and about 200 yards from it, artillery being in front, much of it on the road'; men had not eaten since morning of the 2d; held 'till about 3.20 p.m.'; cannonade 'about one hour' each side, 'my men ... suffered comparatively little, probably less than a dozen men killed and wounded. The brigade lying on my right (Kemper's) suffered severely'; 'Our artillery ceased to fire after about one hour. The enemy continued to fire for awhile after ours had ceased'; 'I do not believe a single battery of the enemy had been disabled'; THE ECHELON TRIGGER: 'The advance had not been made more than twenty or thirty minutes, before three staff officers in quick succession (one from the major-general commanding division) gave me orders to advance to the support of Pickett's division'; 'My brigade, about 1,200 in number' (July 3 engaged-strength primary), order R-to-L 9th-10th-11th-8th-14th Ala; 'Not a man of the division that I was ordered to support could I see'; withdrew on his own judgment, retired under artillery fire, reformed on the original line. July 3 loss 204; July 2 loss 577 (four of five regimental commanders wounded July 2: Forney, Pinckard, Sanders w + Fletcher w); campaign total 777 k/w/m of which 257 missing, 'nearly all ... supposed to be killed or wounded ... but few, if any, not wounded, were taken prisoners.' July 2 arc: 7 a.m. into line; 10th/11th Ala wood fight; McLaws formed at right angles ~2 p.m.; advanced when McLaws' left reached him (his 6.20 p.m. clock); two guns + six guns captured; withdrew unsupported.

clockProfile.assessment: His one July 3 clock, held position 'till about 3.20 p.m.', reads against his own +20-30-min-after-step-off trigger statement (canonical ~15:25-15:35 for his advance under CA-J3A-6=15:05): roughly agreeing to slightly early. His July 2 clocks (2 p.m. McLaws artillery duel opening; 6.20 p.m. advance) ride the July 2 chain and are not used here. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Col. David Lang's report (Perry's Florida Brigade, Anderson's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 631-633 (No. 545)
sources.author: David Lang
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 631-633 read in full). Dated July 29, 1863. July 3 keys: at daylight ordered by Anderson to connect his right with Wilcox's left and 'conform my movements during the day to those of his brigade ... I would receive no further orders' (the standing-conformity trigger, EC4); ~7 a.m. moved up with Wilcox's left 'in front and at the foot of the hill upon which the batteries were in position', skirmishers advanced to the next crest; 'remained quietly until nearly 2 p.m., when the batteries opened a furious bombardment ... which lasted till nearly 4 p.m., when Pickett's division ... charged'; the echelon: 'Soon after General Pickett's troops retired behind our position, General Wilcox began to advance, and, in accordance with previous orders to conform to his movements, I moved forward also' (sequence: the supporting advance follows the repulse); advanced under artillery fire without meeting infantry until the woods at the foot of the heights; left flanked, 'certain annihilation', ordered retreat; 2nd Florida colors + a large number cut off and captured (the 16th Vermont's flank action object, cross: us-i-3-3-stannard.md); Capt. McCaslan (AAAG) killed during the retreat; Maj. Moore (2d Fla) wounded and captured, Capt. Gardner (5th Fla) wounded. Strength/loss primary: 'The brigade went into action near 700 strong, and lost ... 455 killed, wounded, and missing' (matches the p. 343 return 33/217/205=455); July 2 charge lost 'about 300' (=> July 3 share ~155 by subtraction, [B]-grade). July 2 arc: 6 p.m. advance on Wilcox's left, 8th Fla colors lost.

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated July 3 clocks, both consistently LATE against the chain: cannonade opens 'nearly 2 p.m.' (canonical 13:07, offset -53) and Pickett charges 'nearly 4 p.m.' (canonical 15:05, offset -55). Joins the -53 late-stated cluster (Longstreet, Smyth) as its sixth member and first Florida witness. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Maj. John T. Jones's report (Pettigrew's brigade, Heth's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 642-644 (No. 550)
sources.author: John T. Jones
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 642-644 read in full). Dated August 9, 1863; Jones (26th NC) commanded the brigade after Marshall fell. July 1: regimental order R-to-L 52d-47th-11th-26th NC; the branch/wheat-field fight; 26th NC 'lost more than half its men killed and wounded', Col. Burgwyn k and Lt. Col. Lane w 'both with the colors'; Col. Leventhorpe (11th) w, Maj. Ross k. July 2 evening: 'moved about a mile to our right, and took position in rear of our batteries, facing the works of the enemy on Cemetery Hill.' July 3 keys: 'remained here until about 12 o'clock on the 3d, when our batteries opened'; 'About 2 o'clock we were ordered to advance'; step-off friction: 'some confusion in the line, owing to the fact that it had been ordered to close in on the right on Pickett's division, while that command gave way to the left. This was soon corrected'; 'When about half across the intervening space, the enemy opened on us a most destructive fire of grape and canister. When within about 250 or 300 yards of the stone wall ... a perfect hail-storm of lead from their small-arms'; 'many had reached the wall, when we received a deadly volley from the left. The whole line on the left had given way'; fell back to the original position in rear of the batteries; 'After this day's fight, but one field officer was left in the brigade. Regiments that went in with colonels came out commanded by lieutenants.' July 14 Falling Waters: Pettigrew mw ('He had received a severe contusion on the hand on the 3d, but would not report off duty').

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated July 3 clocks, both consistently EARLY against the chain: batteries open 'about 12 o'clock' (canonical 13:07, offset +67) and advance 'about 2 o'clock' (canonical 15:05, offset +65 - his brigade stepped off with the division at the cannonade's end). Joins Cowan (+67) at the early-stated pole from the opposite side of the field. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Capt. J. J. Young (QM, 26th North Carolina) to Gov. Zebulon B. Vance, July 4, 1863, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 645-646 (No. 551)
sources.author: J. J. Young
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11. Written NEAR GETTYSBURG on July 4 - the freshest strength-decay primary in the batch (next-day document, not a retrospective report). Keys: 26th NC 'went in with over 800 men ... There came out but 216, all told, unhurt' (July 1); 'Yesterday they were again engaged, and now have only about 80 men for duty' (after July 3); 'Eleven men were shot down the first day with our colors; yesterday they were lost'; division grain: 'Our whole division numbers but only 1,500 or 1,600 effective men, as officially reported ... The division at the beginning numbered about 8,000 effective men' (Heth's division decay, July 4 official-report basis); officer catalogue incl. Burgwyn k (through both lungs), Lane w (neck/jaw/mouth), 'Colonel Marshall and Major Richardson, of the Fifty-second, supposed to be killed'; Capt. Wagg k by grape July 3; Capt. Albright the only unhurt captain, commanding the regiment. Caution: casualty attributions are day-after supposition in places ('supposed to be killed') - document pin for strengths, conflict-carrying for individual fates.

sources.title: Lt. Col. S. G. Shepard's report (Archer's brigade, Heth's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 646-648 (No. 552)
sources.author: Samuel G. Shepard
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 646-648 read in full). Dated August 10, 1863; written from his own observation plus regimental officers' testimony (he states the method). July 1: Archer captured ('very much exhausted with fatigue'), ~75 lost at the creek obstruction; Fry took command. July 2: 'We were not in the engagement of July 2' (first-class negative evidence); night of the 2d moved 'in front of the enemy's works'. July 3 keys: regimental order R-to-L 1st Tenn - 13th Ala - 14th Tenn - 7th Tenn - 5th Ala Bn; THE CONVERGENCE EXECUTED: 'a space of a few hundred yards between the right of Archer's brigade and the left of General Pickett's division when we advanced ... by the time we had advanced a little over half of the way, the right of Archer's touched and connected with Pickett's left. The command was then passed down the line by the officers, "Guide right"' (claim-fry-guide from the executing side); 'Within 180 or 200 yards of his works, we came to a lane inclosed by two stout post and plank fences. This was a very great obstruction' (the Emmitsburg road on the Fry front); the color record: every flag in the brigade except the 7th Tennessee's captured at or within the works, color-bearer sequences 3/3/4/3 shot down, the 7th's flag saved by Capt. Norris tearing it from the staff; '7 field officers ... went into the charge, only 2 of whom came out'; strength/loss primary: 'We went into the fight on the 1st with 1,048 men, 677 of whom were killed, wounded, and captured during these engagements' (matches the p. 344 return exactly). Falling Waters clubbed-guns fight; Pettigrew mw while with the brigade.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks anywhere in the report; all July 3 facts are sequence-grade (tier D) against CA-J3A-6..9. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Joseph R. Davis's reports (Davis's brigade July 1; Heth's Division July 3), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 648-651 (No. 553)
sources.author: Joseph R. Davis
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 648-651, both reports read in full). Brigade report (Aug 26): July 1 with three regiments (11th Miss LEFT AS DIVISION TRAIN GUARD - EC2 detachment primary), 42d Miss right / 2d Miss center / 55th NC left, the railroad-cut fight, retired ~1 p.m., renewed ~3 p.m. with Ewell's appearance; 'of 9 field officers present, but 2 escaped unhurt.' DIVISION report (Aug 22, written by Davis because Pettigrew was mw at Falling Waters before reporting): evening July 2 line Archer(Fry) right - Pettigrew(Marshall) right center - Davis left center - Brockenbrough left, 'immediately in the rear of our artillery ... the enemy's line ... nearly 1 mile distant, the intervening space ... fields, intersected by strong post and rail fences'; early July 3 morning shells caused 'a few casualties ... in one of the brigades'; 'About 9 a.m. the division was moved to the left about a quarter of a mile, and in the same order of battle was formed in the rear of Major Pegram's battalion' (the morning leg + the Pegram frame anchor); 'About 1 p.m. the artillery along our entire line opened ... For two hours the fire was heavy and incessant ... In Davis' brigade 2 men were killed and 21 wounded' (bombardment apportionment primary); the standing order ('when the artillery in our front ceased firing, the division would attack ... keeping dressed to the right ... in line with ... Pickett's division ... march obliquely to the left'); 'The artillery ceased firing at 3 o'clock, and the order to move forward was given' (the CSA-side twin of Hall's 'At 3 o'clock exactly'); 'Not a gun was fired at us until we reached a strong post and rail fence about three-quarters of a mile from the enemy's position, when we were met by a heavy fire of grape, canister, and shell' (EC5 range relation); 'About 4 p.m. the division reached the line held in the morning'; officer record: 'Colonel B. D. Fry ... and Colonel James K. Marshall ... were wounded and taken prisoners while gallantly leading their brigades' (CONFLICT: the register and Young's July 4 letter carry Marshall killed - both readings recorded); 'in Archer's brigade but two field officers escaped, in Pettigrew's but one, and in Davis' all were killed or wounded'; Pettigrew's horse killed, slight hand wound.

clockProfile.assessment: Three stated July 3 clocks, all agreeing with the chain: cannonade opens 'about 1 p.m.' (canonical 13:07, +7), 'ceased firing at 3 o'clock' (CA-J3A-5 adopted 15:00, +0) with the step-off order immediately following (CA-J3A-6 15:05), retreat complete 'about 4 p.m.'. The best CSA-side chain-agreeing report clock found to date - the Confederate twin of Hall's +5 profile.

sources.title: Maj. Joseph A. Engelhard's report (operations of Pender's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 656-660 (No. 556)
sources.author: Joseph A. Engelhard (AAG, the division report)
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 656-660 read in full). Dated November 4, 1863, written by the AAG because both division commanders (Pender, then Trimble) were lost. July 1: Perrin/Scales assault of Seminary Ridge ('Every field officer, with one exception, was either killed or wounded' in Scales's at the 75-yard bottom; Scales and Riddick w; Pender personally rallied the brigade). July 2: Pender mw late afternoon by shell fragment on his extreme right; command to Lane ~sunset; the Ewell-coordinated night attack prepared then abandoned. July 3 keys - THE TRIMBLE COMMAND RECORD: morning, Lane ordered by Hill to report with his two second-line brigades to Longstreet 'as a support to Pettigrew'; Longstreet ordered them 'in rear of the right of Heth's division'; 'General Lane was relieved of the command by Major General I. R. Trimble, who acted under the same orders' (Trimble assumes command on the field, morning July 3); 'with Lowrance on the right'; 'suffering no little from the two hours' exposure to the heavy artillery fire which preceded the attack'; the advance '~1 mile' with Trimble 'taking immediate command of the movement'; Lane's left uncovered by the front division gaining ground right, its faster advance 'checked by an order from General Trimble'; the division's right 'had reached the works'; the two left regiments of Lane's (Avery, Barry) 'advanced some minutes after the whole line had given way, and fell back, under direct orders'; TRIMBLE PIN: 'severely wounded near the enemy's works, in the leg, which necessitated its amputation, and in the retreat to the Potomac ... fell into the hands of the enemy'; one of his staff k, 2 w; the division reformed by Lane per Trimble's orders 'just in rear of the artillery, and upon the same ground where it had rested.' Scales's brigade context: 'one-half of its remaining numbers killed and wounded in the attack on the 1st'.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks ('two hours' exposure' is a duration, consumed as tier B input); all ordering facts are sequence-grade. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope. Four-month retrospective by a staff officer consolidating brigade reports.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. James H. Lane's report (Lane's brigade, Pender's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 664-667 (No. 558)
sources.author: James H. Lane
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 664-667 read in full). Dated August 13, 1863. July 1: order 7th-37th-28th-18th-33d NC; advanced past the woods 'subsequently occupied by Pegram's battalion'; halted at the McMillan peach orchard/stone fence by Pender's order. July 2: 33d and 18th moved 'to the left of Lieutenant-Colonel Garnett's battalion of artillery' for shelter (frame cross: csa-3c-bn-garnett); skirmishers took the road in front; ~sunset informed of Pender's fall and took division command; the abandoned night attack (his dispositions given in detail). July 3 keys: ordered by Hill 'to move in person to the right, with the two brigades forming my second line, and to report to General Longstreet as a support to Pettigrew. General Longstreet ordered me to form in rear of the right of Heth's division'; 'putting Lowrance on the right'; relieved by Trimble; STRUCTURAL STATEMENT: 'Heth's division was much larger than Lowrance's brigade and my own, which were its only support, and there was consequently no second line in rear of its left'; the advance 'through the woods in which our batteries were planted, and through an open field about a mile'; 'As soon as Pettigrew's command gave back, Lowrance's brigade and my own, without ever having halted, took position on the left of the troops which were still contesting the ground'; 'repeatedly driving the cannoneers from their pieces, completely silencing the guns in our immediate front'; 'We advanced to within a few yards of the stone wall, exposed all the while to a heavy raking artillery fire from the right. My left was here very much exposed, and a column of the enemy's infantry was thrown forward in that direction, which enfiladed my whole line' (the 8th Ohio object, cross: Smyth/Woodruff frames); withdrew, 'reformed immediately in rear of the artillery, as directed by General Trimble'; STRENGTH/LOSS PRIMARY: '660 out of an effective total of 1,355, including ambulance corps and rear guard, our loss on the 1st and 2nd being but slight' (vs the p. 344 return's 389 k+w with the OR's own footnote carrying his 660 - the missing difference, ED-47 candidate); Trimble wounded -> Lane again commanded the division from July 3 evening.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks; sequence-grade throughout. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Alfred M. Scales's report (Scales's brigade, July 1), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 669-670 (No. 560)
sources.author: Alfred M. Scales
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11. Dated August 14, 1863; covers July 1 only (Scales w that evening, refers the rest to Lowrance). Keys: the brigade's July 1 charge past the halted exhausted front line ('without ammunition, and would not advance farther'), the descent opposite the seminary, 'a most terrific fire of grape and shell on our flank, and grape and musketry in our front' to the bottom ~75 yards out; 'Here I received a painful wound from a piece of shell'; 'Every field officer of the brigade save one had been disabled'; HIS CASUALTY TABLE (printed in the report): officers 9k/45w/1m=55, men 39/336/115=490, TOTAL 48/381/116=545 - CONFLICT with the p. 345 return's brigade total 102/323/110=535 for July 1-3 (a July-1-heavy report table larger than the compiled three-day total; both readings carried, never averaged; the return itself warns its totals are approximative).

sources.title: Col. William L. J. Lowrance's report (Scales's brigade from July 1 evening), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 671-673 (No. 561)
sources.author: William Lee J. Lowrance
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 671-672 read in full). Dated August 12, 1863. Keys: took command evening July 1, brigade 'numbering in all about 500 men, without any field officers, excepting Lieutenant-Colonel Gordon and myself ... In this depressed, dilapidated, and almost unorganized condition' (EC2 primary for the July 3 basis); moved at 1 a.m., July 2 dawn to the extreme right 'on line with the artillery ... its only guard' with a half-mile skirmish line, relieved 1 p.m. by Anderson's division, rejoined the division second line right of Lane; July 3 morning with Lane to the right, 'placed under command of General Trimble, and were formed on the second line, in rear of Major Poague's battalion of artillery' (EC3 via the Poague frame); 'here we remained at least one hour, under a most galling fire of artillery'; the advance: 'a wide, hot, and already crimson plain ... at a distance of 1 mile'; 'ere we had advanced over two-thirds of the way, troops from the front came tearing through our ranks, which caused many of our men to break, but with the remaining few we went forward until the right of the brigade touched the enemy's line of breastworks'; THE CLIMAX STRENGTH PIN: 'The two brigades (now reduced to mere squads, not numbering in all 800 guns) were the only line to be seen upon that vast field, and no support in view ... without orders, the brigade retreated'; rallied on the original line; wounded himself July 3 (register: w July 3) - his report does not narrate his own wound.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks ('at least one hour' under fire is a duration input, tier B); sequence-grade. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope.

sources.title: Maj. Gen. Richard H. Anderson's report (Anderson's Division, Third Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 613-616 (No. 536)
sources.author: Richard H. Anderson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 614-616 read). Division line July 2 (R-to-L Wilcox-Perry-Wright-Posey-Mahone), enemy line 'about 1,200 yards in our front'; July 2 en-echelon commitment and withdrawal 'in the same succession in which they had advanced'. July 3 keys: 'at 3.30 o'clock ... troops of General Longstreet's corps were advanced to the assault of the enemy's center'; 'I received orders to hold my division in readiness to move up in support'; 'Wilcox's and Perry's brigades had been moved forward, so as to be in position to render assistance'; THE STOP ORDER: 'I was about to move forward Wright's and Posey's brigades, when Lieutenant-General Longstreet directed me to stop the movement, adding that it was useless, and would only involve unnecessary loss, the assault having failed' (CA-J3A-10 layer, the counter-echelon that never launched); EC6 attribution: 'Some loss was sustained by each of the brigades of the division from the cannonading. Wilcox's, which was supporting Alexander's artillery, suffering the most seriously.' Total division campaign loss 2,266 (p. 616, incl. Manassas Gap).

clockProfile.assessment: One stated July 3 clock: Longstreet's corps advanced 'at 3.30 o'clock' (canonical CA-J3A-6 15:05, offset -25) - a moderately late-stated clock between the agreeing cluster and the -53 pole. His July 2 clocks (2 p.m. artillery, 5.30 McLaws) ride the July 2 chain. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Gen. R. E. Lee to Maj. Gen. George E. Pickett [no date]: destroy the division report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 3 p. 1075
sources.author: Robert E. Lee
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-iii route, id = 190731 + page). Verbatim: 'You and your men have crowned yourselves with glory; but we have the enemy to fight, and must carefully, at this critical moment, guard against dissensions which the reflections in your report would create. I will, therefore, suggest that you destroy both copy and original, substituting one confined to casualties merely. I hope all will yet be well.' Printed undated among August 1863 correspondence. THE negative-evidence primary for why no Pickett division report exists in the OR (No. 441 Peyton and No. 442 Dearing are the division's only surviving reports); the suppressed report itself has never been produced (LaSalle Corbell Pickett's claims never verified).
